message.reasoning_content:
We are asked to fill the two candidate stubs: J0136+2425 and HVS 8 from paper 2101.10878. We need to provide complete CandidateRecord (the candidate fields: identifiers, inclusion_assessment, candidate_origin, core, photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, abundances, quality_flags, orbit, astrophysical_origin, extra). The scaffold already has some fields for the overall extraction (method_chain, extraction, etc.) but we need to fill these two candidates.

We have the paper and ECSV tables. Let's extract data.

First, for J0136+2425 (candidate number 14 in tables). The paper describes it as "whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." (line 176-177). But earlier, line 97: "the potential hyper-runaway stars SDSS J013655.91+242546.0 (J0136+2425 for short)". So it is considered as a potential hyper-runaway star, i.e., possibly unbound. In Table 1, its ejection velocity is 500 ± 50 km/s, rp 14.2, etc. The text does not explicitly call it unbound for mass model I (the one used in Table 3), but in Table 3 we can check its bound probability Pb. In the kinematics table, row 14 shows Pb = 40%? Actually line 706 col 012? Let's check: Table 3 (table-table-kinematics_AS.ecsv). Row 14 (corresponds to candidate number 14) line 56: "#" col_001 = 14, then col_002 to col_023. For Pb, col_012 (P_b) is value 40 (in the main row), then uncertainties row is "..." but no uncertainty given? Actually line 57: col_012 in uncertainty row: "..." (no number). So Pb = 40% means 40% bound, so unbound probability 60%. The paper does not explicitly say it is unbound, but says "whether ... are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So for model I, Pb=40%, so it's not bound (i.e., likely unbound). Thus it counts as possibly unbound, so inclusion assessment with possibly_unbound. Galactic bound claim: "possibly_unbound". Inclusion basis: "explicit_unbound_text"? Actually the paper discusses them as hyper-runaway candidates. The sentence: "Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." This acknowledges that they might be unbound. We'll classify as "possibly_unbound" and inclusion_basis "explicit_candidate_text". Paper labels: ["hvs_candidate", "hyper_runaway_candidate"] perhaps. The text mentions "potential hyper-runaway stars". So labels: hvs_candidate, hyper_runaway_candidate. Also maybe runaway_candidate. We'll pick appropriate.

For HVS 8 (candidate 18). In Table 3 row 18, Pb = 55%? Actually line 714: col_012 is 55? Let's find HVS 8 in kinematics table: candidate number 18 corresponds to HVS 8. In table-table-kinematics_AS.ecsv, row 18 starts at line 714: col_001 = 18, then col_002 etc up to col_023. col_012 (P_b) is value 55 (in main row), uncertainties line has "..."? line 715: col_012 is "..."? Actually line 715 shows " ..." for col_012? Checking: line 714: after col_005 etc. I'll reconstruct: the line: "18 $-29.5$ $-13.0$ $26.1$ $41.5$ &   & $-380$ $18$ $261$ $460$ $-5$ $55$ $5$ $-10.7$ $0.0$ $11.6$ &   & $-430$ $-120$ $367$ $575$ $427$ $82$". So Pb=55, meaning 55% bound, 45% unbound. Also text says "whether ... HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So also possibly unbound.

So we'll treat both as possibly unbound candidates.

Now fill candidate records.

We need to extract all core quantities (observed_phase_space, derived_kinematics, bound_assessment) from tables.

Observed phase space: RA and Dec come from Gaia EDR3 identifiers but the paper doesn't give RA/Dec explicitly. However the table 2 gives Gaia EDR3 source IDs. Can we infer RA/Dec from the Gaia DR3 catalog? We are not supposed to download external data. The paper does not give RA/Dec in the tables. We can use the Gaia EDR3 identifier as stored in the paper. The schema allows gaia_source_id. But for RA and Dec we need to fill CoordinateQuantityRecord? However the paper does not provide RA/Dec values. So we can leave ra and dec null? The schema says they are optional? In ObservedPhaseSpace all fields are optional (nullable). So we can omit ra, dec, if not given. But we could perhaps get them from external catalogs? The instructions say "Do not download or parse new external resources." So we cannot look up Gaia coordinates. The paper does not list RA/Dec in tables. So we'll set ra and dec as null. In the candidate's phase space, we have distance (from spectrophotometry) and proper motions and radial velocity. Proper motions are not listed in the tables either. They are from Gaia EDR3 but the paper doesn't show them. The kinematics table gives derived galactocentric coordinates. So we cannot fill proper motions. So we'll leave all observed phase space fields as null except maybe radial velocity and distance. Radial velocity: from table 2 (spectrophotometry) column col_006 (v_rad) for each star. Distance: col_012 (d) in table 2. So we have radial_velocity and distance.

Parallax: not provided, can omit.

Proper motions: not given.

Thus observed_phase_space will have distance and radial_velocity maybe.

Distance: from table 2, row for candidate 14: col_012 is "10.9^{+2.0}_{-2.0}" (kpc). So we can fill distance with raw_value "$10.9^{+2.0}_{-2.0}$", value 10.9, error? The format is asymmetric: upper_error: 2.0, lower_error: 2.0? Actually it's symmetric. We'll put error: 2.0, or lower/upper both 2.0. The unit: kpc.

Radial velocity: col_006: "324.3^{+5.9}_{-5.9}", so v_rad = 324.3 km/s, symmetric uncertainty 5.9.

So observed_phase_space: distance and radial_velocity.

Derived kinematics: from table 3 (kinematics) we have galactocentric x,y,z, v_x, v_y, v_z, v_Grf, v_Grf - v_esc, P_b, plane-crossing positions and velocities. So we can fill derived_kinematics fields: galactocentric_x, y, z, galactocentric_vx, vy, vz, total_velocity (v_Grf) or galactocentric_tangential? Actually table gives v_Grf as total galactocentric rest-frame velocity. So we can put galactocentric_vx etc, and total_velocity (as v_Grf). The tangential velocity is not given, skip. Also galactocentric_radius (r) is given as r (col_005). So fill those.

Bound assessment: escape_velocity (v_esc) can be derived from v_Grf - v_esc difference? Actually table gives v_Grf - v_esc as col_011. So we can compute v_esc = v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc). But we need to extract both. So we'll fill escape_velocity as a computed quantity maybe from paper? The paper doesn't give v_esc directly. But we can calculate from columns: v_Grf (col_010) minus col_011 gives v_esc. However the schema might want escape_velocity as a quantity. We could fill escape_velocity_ratio maybe. But better to fill bound_probability (P_b) and unbound_probability (100 - Pb). Also escape_margin: v_Grf - v_esc. We can fill escape_margin from col_011, and also bound_status_metric? The probability gives the bound status. So bound_assessment will have escape_margin (raw value from col_011), bound_probability (Pb normalized to fraction) and unbound_probability (1 - Pb). We can infer escape_velocity = v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc). But we need to compute value from table values. Let's get exact numbers:

For candidate 14:
From row 14 (line 56-57):
col_002 (x) = -14.6, uncertainty: ^+1.2_-1.2
col_003 (y) = 6.0, ^+1.1_-1.2
col_004 (z) = -6.6, ^+1.3_-1.2
col_005 (r) = 17.2, ^+1.8_-1.9
col_007 (v_x) = -10, ^+40_-40
col_008 (v_y) = 295, ^+26_-26
col_009 (v_z) = -470, ^+60_-60
col_010 (v_Grf) = 551, ^+33_-27
col_011 (v_Grf - v_esc) = 10, ^+50_-40
col_012 (P_b) = 40, no error
col_013 (x_p) = -14.1, ^+1.5_-1.6
col_014 (y_p) = 1.8, ^+1.2_-1.1
col_015 (z_p) = 0.0, ^+0.2_-0.1
col_016 (r_p) = 14.2, ^+1.8_-1.6
col_018 (v_x,p) = -50, ^+40_-40
col_019 (v_y,p) = 307, ^+24_-25
col_020 (v_z,p) = -490, ^+60_-60
col_021 (v_Grf,p) = 570, ^+30_-25
col_022 (v_ej) = 500, ^+50_-50
col_023 (tau_flight) = 13.7, ^+0.9_-1.2

So derived kinematics: galactocentric x,y,z, radius, v_x, v_y, v_z, total_velocity (v_Grf). We'll use these as core. The bound_assessment: escape_margin = 10, bound_probability: Pb=40% => 0.40, unbound_probability: 0.60. escape_velocity: v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) = 551 - 10 = 541 km/s, but that's the escape velocity at that position? Actually the table gives v_Grf - v_esc as difference, so v_esc = v_Grf - that value = 541 km/s. But we must check if this is the local escape velocity. We'll put it as a derived quantity.

For HVS 8 (candidate 18):
From row 18 (line 714-715):
col_002 x: -29.5 ^+1.8_-1.8
col_003 y: -13.0 ^+1.1_-1.1
col_004 z: 26.1 ^+2.1_-2.3
col_005 r: 41.5 ^+3.0_-3.0
col_007 v_x: -380 ^+27_-26
col_008 v_y: 18 ^+22_-23
col_009 v_z: 261 ^+19_-19
col_010 v_Grf: 460 ^+16_-14
col_011 v_Grf - v_esc: -5 ^+19_-16
col_012 P_b: 55
col_013 x_p: 5 ^+5_-4
col_014 y_p: -10.7 ^+1.8_-1.9
col_015 z_p: 0.0 ^+0.1_-0.1
col_016 r_p: 11.6 ^+3.3_-2.1
col_018 v_x,p: -430 ^+10_-10
col_019 v_y,p: -120 ^+29_-29
col_020 v_z,p: 367 ^+11_-15
col_021 v_Grf,p: 575 ^+9_-8
col_022 v_ej: 427 ^+13_-7
col_023 tau_flight: 82 ^+10_-9

So derived kinematics similar. bound_assessment: escape_margin = -5, bound_probability = 55% -> 0.55, unbound_probability = 0.45. escape_velocity = v_Grf - margin = 460 - (-5) = 465 km/s.

Now for photometry, spectroscopy, stellar parameters, abundances, quality_flags: The paper gives atmospheric and stellar parameters in Table 2. We can extract T_eff, log_g, vsini, spectrograph, mass, radius, log L/L_sun, evolutionary age, distance. Also radial velocity is already in observed phase. We can put photometry? Not given in tables. Spectroscopy: we have spectrograph instrument and possibly spectral type (but not given). So we'll fill stellar_parameters (teff, log_g, mass, radius, luminosity, evolutionary age, and perhaps vsini could be a quality flag or stellar parameter? vsini is rotational velocity, can be put as extra or in stellar_parameters other. But stellar_parameters object has no vsini field. So we can put vsini in extra. Also spectral_type not given. So we'll fill stellar_parameters with teff, log_g, mass, radius, and luminosity (log L/L_sun). Age as well. and maybe other fields. The schema allows age. So we'll do that.

Photometry: none. Spectroscopy: we can put a record for spectrograph/radial_velocity follow-up. The spectrograph and resolution is given as "S (2000)" for J0136+2425 (from Table 2 row 14, col_007 = "S (2000)") and for HVS 8 (ID 18): col_007 = "M (1.2 Å)". So we can add spectroscopy entries for the instrument/survey. For Spectrograph, we can encode measurement_type "spectral_type" maybe not, but we can set measurement_type "other" or "classification". But schema SpectroscopyRecord has measurement_type enum: spectral_type, radial_velocity_follow_up, line_measurement, signal_to_noise, classification, other. We could put "classification" for spectrograph. We'll create a spectroscopy record with measurement_type "other" and note the instrument. Also radial velocity from spectra can be considered radial_velocity_follow_up? The observed radial velocity is already in observed_phase_space, but we could note the instrument. We'll keep radial_velocity in observed_phase_space and not duplicate. So we'll add spectroscopy for instrument.

Abundances: none given.

Quality flags: none.

Orbit: we have pericenter/apocenter? Not given. The tables give r_p (pericenter distance), maybe that is pericenter? r_p is galactocentric radius at plane intersection, but that's not orbit pericenter. The orbit integration yields the plane-crossing distances, which are not orbital elements. We don't have eccentricity, apocenter, etc. So orbit fields might be empty. But we have flight time (tau_flight) which is an orbital quantity. We could put flight_time in orbit record. Also maybe other fields like pericenter? Not. We'll put flight_time as extra or in orbit flight_time. The orbit flight_time is available. We could also put pericenter as r_p? But r_p is plane-crossing radius, not the orbit's pericenter. However the paper says "galactocentric radius at plane intersection" is the star's distance from GC when crossing the plane. That's not pericenter unless the orbit is radial. But we could put it as pericenter if the star originates at the plane and is moving away? Not sure. Better to leave orbit empty, but we can add flight_time.

Astrophysical origin: origin_site, origin_classification, ejection_velocity, travel_time (which is flight_time), hypothesis_metrics. The paper assigns origin flags: "D" for disk runaway, "R" for outer rim. For J0136+2425, flag "D" (disk). For HVS 8, flag "D". We can set origin_classification to "disk runaway". Also ejection_velocity from table (v_ej). We'll put ejection_velocity quantity. Travel_time: flight_time.

Also we have the flag F from Table 1: C, D, R. The paper describes: "The flag in the last column marks objects that potentially originate in the GC (``C''), are bona fide disk runaways (``D''), or stem from the outer rim of the disk (``R'')." J0136+2425 has flag D, HVS 8 has flag D. So origin_classification can be "disk runaway star".

Now inclusion assessment details:

For J0136+2425: The paper mentions it as potential hyper-runaway star (line 97: "the potential hyper-runaway stars SDSS J013655.91+242546.0 (J0136+2425 for short)"). In the discussion (line 176-177): "Whether LAMOST HVS 2, J0136+2425, and HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So it's clearly considered as possibly unbound. So galactic_bound_claim: "possibly_unbound". Paper labels: ["hvs_candidate", "hyper_runaway_candidate", "runaway_candidate"]? The term "hyper-runaway star" is used. So we'll add "hyper_runaway_candidate". Also "high_velocity_star"? Not explicitly. Inclusion_basis: "explicit_candidate_text" because it's stated as a potential hyper-runaway star. Extraction_confidence: high, because the text is clear. Source refs: we'll cite the lines.

For HVS 8: similarly, described in text as possibly unbound. The phrase "whether ... HVS 8 are hyper-runaway stars depends on the choice of the applied Galactic mass model." So same labels: hvs_candidate, hyper_runaway_candidate. Also the paper earlier refers to "hyper-runaway stars" as those that are unbound but not from the GC. HVS 5 and HVS 6 are explicitly unbound; HVS 8 is borderline. So inclusion_basis: explicit_candidate_text. Extraction_confidence: high.

candidate_origin: For both stars, they were previously known as HVS candidates or runaway candidates? J0136+2425 was originally a potential hyper-runaway star from Tillich et al. 2009. So it is cited_from_literature. The paper says: "the potential hyper-runaway stars SDSS J013655.91+242546.0 (J0136+2425 for short, Tillich et al. 2009)". So origin_type: cited_from_literature, and paper_reassesses_unbound_status: true (they reassess based on Gaia EDR3 and new analysis). We need to fill citation. For HVS 8: It's from the MMT-HVS survey (Brown et al. 2014), as part of HVS candidates. The paper mentions that the sample includes 40 stars from MMT-HVS survey revised by Kreuzer et al. 2020. So HVS 8 is among those. So cited_from_literature, reassesses. We'll need bibliography refs. The bibliography entries: Tillich et al. 2009 -> bibkey "2009A&A...507L..37T". For HVS 8, the original HVS candidate comes from Brown et al. 2014, bibcode "2014ApJ...787...89B". In the text they cite \citeads{2014ApJ...787...89B} and \citeads{2020A&A...637A..53K}. So we'll set citation accordingly.

Now fill identifiers: both stubs already have identifiers from the roster. But we need to add paper_candidate_id as "J0136+2425" and "HVS 8". Already present. Also gaia_source_id as given. All array with both names. So okay.

Now we need to extract exact values with source_refs from ECSV cells. We'll use ecsv_cell references. For each quantity, we'll point to the ECSV file line and column. The ECSV files have line numbers as given. We'll use the line numbers of the data rows. For J0136+2425: In table-table-vejection.ecsv, row for candidate 14 is line 26: "J0136 + 2425" 14 500^+50_-50 14.2^+1.8_-1.6 2.45^+0.20_-0.20 D. So we can extract ejection velocity, r_p, mass from that table. In table-table-spectrophotometry.ecsv, row for #14 is line 44: col_001 = 14, col_002 = Gaia EDR3 291821209329550464, col_003 = "\phantom09 100^+250_-250" (T_eff), etc. We'll extract T_eff, log_g, vsini, v_rad, spectrograph, mass, radius, logL, tau_evol, d. In table-table-kinematics_AS.ecsv, row 14 is line 56-57. So we'll use those lines.

For HVS 8: candidate 18. Table 1 line 30: "HVS 8" 18 427^+13_-\phantom07 11.6^+3.3_-2.1 2.93^+0.08_-0.07 D. Table 2 line 48: row 18, col_002 = Gaia EDR3 633599760258827776, col_003 = 10 880^+120_-120, ... Table 3 line 714-715.

We need to ensure raw_value captures the exact string from the cell, including LaTeX commands like \varv, or \phantom. The ECSV file has already processed LaTeX? It seems the ECSV cells contain the cleaned text without LaTeX? For example, in table-table-vejection.ecsv, the raw value for col_003 is "1810^+60_-60" which is plain text. So we can use that. In table-table-kinematics_AS.ecsv, values like "-14.6" are plain, but there are ^+..._-... in separate rows for uncertainties. We'll handle appropriately: for main values we take the line 56 for candidate 14, and for uncertainties we need the second line. The quantity record for galactocentric_x will have value = -14.6, error not directly; we could set lower/upper error from the uncertainty row. So we'll put lower_error = 1.2, upper_error = 1.2? But the paper gives symmetric uncertainties? The uncertainties are asymmetric as ^+1.2_-1.2 meaning upper and lower are equal, but for some they are different (e.g., col_011 for candidate 14: ^+50_-40). So we'll extract lower_error and upper_error separately. The schema QuantityRecord has lower_error and upper_error as strings, so we can put the numeric values. The raw_value for the uncertainty row could be given separately? But we can combine the main and uncertainty rows references. For each field, we'll include source_refs pointing to the main value line, and maybe we note the uncertainty line separately. But the uncertainty is part of the same cell pair. We'll capture both lines for error ranges. For simplicity, we can include an ecsv_cell ref for the main value row and another for the uncertainty row, or just include the main row and extract numeric uncertainties from the uncertainty row. I'll do two source_refs for the quantity: one for the central value row, one for the uncertainty row. But the schema source_refs is an array of refs. So we can include both.

Now method_refs for each quantity: We need to map the quantity to a step in method_chain. All derived kinematics come from velocity_calculation (step-07) and orbit (maybe step-08) but Galactocentric positions and velocities are from velocity_calculation. The bound probabilities and escape margins from escape_or_bound_assessment (step-09). Distances from distance_estimation (step-02). Radial velocities from radial_velocity_measurement (step-03). Stellar parameters from stellar_parameter_inference? The paper describes spectrophotometric analysis giving T_eff, log g, etc. That's step-02 but not explicitly separated; step-02 includes distance estimation and stellar parameter inference? The skill says we should have separate step for stellar_parameter_inference. However the paper's method just does a combined spectral analysis to derive atmospheric and stellar parameters, then uses those to compute distance. The method chain we have step-02 is distance_estimation, but that step also produces stellar parameters. Could we treat the same step? The schema step_type could be "stellar_parameter_inference" separate? The paper's analysis step includes atmospheric parameters, so we could have a step stellar_parameter_inference that feeds distance_estimation. But our scaffold doesn't have such a step. I think we can adjust the method chain later, but the current scaffold has only distance_estimation and radial_velocity_measurement. The atmospheric parameters come from the same spectral fitting as distance, so they could be output from step-02. For direct method_refs, we'll set radial_velocity to step-03, distance to step-02, stellar parameters to step-02 (or we could create a separate step, but we can note it as step-02). The scaffold's method_chain is not being edited now, we are only filling candidates. So for distance we use step-02, for radial velocity step-03, for stellar parameters (T_eff, log g, mass, radius, luminosity, age) we could step-02 (distance_estimation that also produced stellar parameters) or we could step-04 (cross_match) but that just combines. Actually the spectroscopic analysis determines atmospheric parameters and stellar parameters, so that is step-02. We'll assign step-02 for those, even though step-02 is named distance_estimation. The paper says "Quantitative spectrophotometric analysis" which includes both atmospheric parameters and distance. So okay. For photometry (not present) we would need photometric_or_sed_modeling, but we don't have photometry.

For galactocentric quantities, we use step-07 (velocity_calculation). For bound assessment, step-09. For orbit integration outputs like flight time, step-08. For origin assessment (ejection velocity, r_p) step-10. We'll assign accordingly.

Now we'll fill the candidate JSON.

Let's start building.

We'll produce a JSON object {"candidates": [cand1, cand2]}.

We'll write concise but complete.

I need to include coordinate frames for RA/Dec if present, but we are not providing RA/Dec, so skip.

Now for each candidate, we'll fill:

identifiers (already defined, fill as per stub, ensure record_id matches "2101.10878:cand-009" and "2101.10878:cand-010").

Then inclusion_assessment, candidate_origin, core, photometry, spectroscopy, stellar_parameters, abundances, quality_flags, orbit, astrophysical_origin, extra.

We'll need to format raw values exactly from ECSV cells.

For J0136+2425:

- inclusion_assessment:
  summary: "Previously proposed potential hyper-runaway star; paper discusses unbound status depending on Galactic mass model (model I gives bound probability 40%)."
  paper_labels: ["hvs_candidate", "hyper_runaway_candidate", "runaway_candidate"]
  galactic_bound_claim: "possibly_unbound"
  inclusion_basis: "explicit_candidate_text"
  extraction_confidence: "high"
  confidence_reason: "The paper explicitly mentions it as a hyper-runaway candidate and discusses its possible unbound status."
  source_refs: [text ref lines 97, 176-177]

- candidate_origin:
  origin_type: "cited_from_literature"
  paper_reassesses_unbound_status: true
  source_refs: [text ref lines 97, 176-177]
  citation: bibkey "2009A&A...507L..37T", with citation_context_refs pointing to the text where the paper cites Tillich et al. 2009. The text cites \citeads{2009A&A...507L..37T} in line 97? Actually the sentence "the potential hyper-runaway stars SDSS J013655.91+242546.0 (J0136+2425 for short, Tillich et al. 2009)" includes a citation. In the source, it's \citeads{2009A&A...507L..37T}? The tex file at line 97: "the potential hyper-runaway stars SDSS\,J013655.91$+$242546.0 (J0136$+$2425 for short, \citeads{2009A&A...507L..37T})". So we'll cite that line. bibliography_refs: the bibitem should be the entry from thebibliography. In the paper, line 586-589: the bibitem for Tillich et al. 2009. So we'll reference that.

- core:
  observed_phase_space:
    distance: from table 2 row col_012: raw "$10.9^{+2.0}_{-2.0}$", value 10.9, error: symmetric? The error bars are ^+2.0_-2.0, so upper_error 2.0, lower_error 2.0. We'll put unit "kpc". source_refs: ECSV cell line 44 column col_012.
    radial_velocity: from table 2 row col_006: "$324.3^{+5.9}_{-5.9}$", value 324.3, error 5.9, unit "km/s".
  derived_kinematics:
    galactocentric_x: from table 3 col_002: "-14.6", lower_error 1.2, upper_error 1.2, unit "kpc". source_refs main row line 56, uncertainty line 57.
    similarly y: 6.0 +1.1 -1.2 (lower 1.2, upper 1.1? Actually table uncertainty: ^+1.1_-1.2 meaning upper 1.1, lower 1.2. So we'll put lower_error 1.2, upper_error 1.1.
    z: -6.6 +1.3 -1.2 -> lower 1.2, upper 1.3
    galactocentric_radius: r = 17.2 +1.8 -1.9 -> lower 1.9, upper 1.8
    galactocentric_vx: -10 +40 -40 -> symmetric 40
    vy: 295 +26 -26
    vz: -470 +60 -60
    total_velocity (galactic_rest_frame_velocity? The schema has total_velocity and galactocentric_tangential_velocity? Actually DerivedKinematics has total_velocity and galactocentric_tangential_velocity. We'll put total_velocity as v_Grf = 551 +33 -27. So total_velocity raw "$551^{+33}_{-27}$", value 551, lower_error 27? Actually ^+33_-27 means upper error +33, lower error -27. So lower_error 27? But lower_error field is positive? We'll put lower_error 27, upper_error 33. But the uncertainty notation: lower error is the absolute value, so we can store +33 for upper_error and +27 for lower_error? The schema expects string, we can put "33" and "27". So for lower_error we put 27, upper_error 33? Actually the field lower_error is for the negative side, we can put 27 (positive number). Similarly.
  bound_assessment:
    escape_velocity: not directly given but computed: v_Grf - (v_Grf - v_esc) = 551 - 10 = 541 km/s. But the paper does not provide this value explicitly, so we cannot extract as a paper value. Should we omit? The schema does not require escape_velocity, it's optional. We could compute from table? The skill says "Do not normalize units by recomputing values unless the paper explicitly gives the converted value." So we should not compute escape_velocity. So we exclude escape_velocity. Instead we have escape_margin and bound probabilities.
    escape_margin: from col_011 raw "$10^{+50}_{-40}$", value 10, lower_error 40, upper_error 50, unit "km/s". Actually the value is v_Grf - v_esc, so positive means above escape velocity? The paper says v_Grf - v_esc. So margin positive => unbound, negative => bound. In this case margin 10, so slightly above escape. So we fill escape_margin.
    bound_probability: P_b = 40% => raw "40", value 0.40, unit "".
    unbound_probability: value 0.60 (100-Pb). But we don't have direct paper value for unbound probability. The paper only gives bound probability. Could we infer unbound as 1 - Pb? That is a trivial computation. The schema's unbound_probability is not required, but we can fill it if we want to explicitly express.
